Nicodemus was afraid, or ashamed to be seen with Christ, so he came during the night. When religious belief is out of favor, there are many like Nicodemus. But even though he came at night, Jesus welcomed him and taught us to encourage new beginnings, even if they are weak. And although he initially came by night, later he openly acknowledged Christ. He did not talk with Christ about political matters, even though he was a leader, but instead about the concerns of his own soul and its salvation, and went directly to those topics. Our Savior spoke about the necessity and nature of being made new or born again, and immediately directed Nicodemus to the source of holiness in the heart. Birth is the beginning of life; to be born again is to begin living anew, like someone who has lived poorly or without purpose. We must have a new nature, new principles, new feelings, new goals. Through our first birth, we were corrupted and shaped by sin; therefore, we must become new creations. No stronger expression could have been chosen to signify such a great and remarkable change in state and character. We must be entirely different from what we were before, because something that begins to exist at one time cannot be the same as something that existed previously. This new birth comes from heaven, and it leads toward heaven. It is a significant change made in the heart of a sinner by the power of the Holy Spirit. It means that something is done within us and for us that we cannot do ourselves. Something is wrong, by which such a life begins as will last forever. We cannot otherwise expect any benefit from Christ; it is necessary to our happiness here and hereafter. What Christ said, Nicodemus misunderstood, as if there were no other way of renewing and reshaping an immortal soul than by reshaping the body. But he acknowledged his ignorance, which shows a desire to be better informed. It was then further explained by the Lord Jesus, who showed the source of this blessed change. It is not accomplished by any wisdom or power of our own, but by the power of the Holy Spirit. We are shaped in sin, which makes it necessary that our nature be changed. We should not be surprised at this; for when we consider God’s holiness, the corruption of our nature, and the happiness set before us, we will not think it strange that so much importance is placed on this. The regenerating work of the Holy Spirit is compared to water. It is also likely that Christ had reference to the ordinance of baptism. Not all who are baptized are saved; but without that new birth which is accomplished by the Spirit and signified by baptism, none will be subjects of the kingdom of heaven. The same word signifies both the wind and the Spirit. The wind blows where it pleases for us; God directs it. The Spirit sends his influences where, when, on whom, and in what measure and degree he chooses. Though the causes are hidden, the effects are plain when the soul is brought to mourn for sin and yearn for Christ. It seemed that Christ’s explanation of the doctrine and necessity of being made new did not make it any clearer to Nicodemus. Thus, the things of God are foolishness to the natural person. Many think they cannot prove what they cannot believe. Christ's discourse about gospel truths shows the folly of those who consider these matters strange; and it encourages us to investigate them. Jesus Christ is fully able to reveal the will of God to us; for he came down from heaven, and yet remains in heaven. Here we have a notice of Christ’s two distinct natures in one person, so that while he is the Son of man, he is also in heaven. God is “HE THAT IS,” and heaven is the dwelling place of his holiness. The knowledge of this must come from above, and can be received by faith alone. Jesus Christ came to save us by healing us, as the children of Israel, stung with fiery serpents, were cured and lived by looking up to the bronze serpent. In this, observe the deadly and destructive nature of sin. Ask those who have a guilty conscience, ask condemned sinners, they will tell you that even though the allurements of sin may be appealing, it ultimately bites like a serpent. See the powerful remedy against this fatal illness. Christ is plainly presented to us in the gospel. He whom we offended is our Peace, and the way of seeking a cure is by believing. If anyone disregards either their disease through sin or the method of cure through Christ, and does not accept Christ on his own terms, their ruin will be their own fault. He has said, Look and be saved, look and live; lift up the eyes of your faith to Christ crucified. And until we have grace to do this, we shall not be cured, but still are wounded by the stings of Satan, and in a dying state. Jesus Christ came to save us by pardoning us, so that we would not die under the sentence of the law. Here is gospel, good news indeed. Here is God’s love in giving his Son for the world. God so loved the world; so truly, so richly. Behold and wonder that the great God should love such a worthless world! Here also is the great gospel duty to believe in Jesus Christ. God having given him to be our Prophet, Priest, and King, we must give up ourselves to be ruled, taught, and saved by him. And here is the great gospel benefit: whoever believes in Christ will not perish but will have everlasting life. God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself, and so saving it. It could not be saved except through him; there is no other salvation. From all this is shown the happiness of true believers; he who believes in Christ is not condemned. Though he has been a great sinner, yet he is not dealt with according to what his sins deserve. How great is the sin of unbelievers! God sent One to save us, someone dearest to himself; and will he not be dearest to us? How great is the misery of unbelievers! they are already condemned; which speaks of a certain condemnation; a present condemnation. The wrath of God now falls upon them; and their own hearts condemn them. There is also a condemnation grounded on their former guilt; they are open to the law for all their sins because they are not by faith interested in the gospel pardon. Unbelief is a sin against the remedy. It springs from the enmity of the heart of man to God, from love of sin in some form. Read also the doom of those who would not know Christ. Sinful works are works of darkness. The wicked world keeps as far from this light as they can, lest their deeds should be reproved. Christ is hated because sin is loved. If they had not hated saving knowledge, they would not sit down contentedly in condemning ignorance. On the other hand, renewed hearts welcome this light. A good person acts truly and sincerely in all he does. He desires to know what the will of God is and to do it, even against his own worldly interest. A change in his whole character and conduct has taken place. The love of God is shed abroad in his heart by the Holy Ghost, and is become the commanding principle of his actions. So long as he continues under a load of unforgiven guilt, there can be little else than slavish fear of God; but when his doubts are done away, when he sees the righteous ground whereon this forgiveness is built, he rests on it as his own, and is united to God by unfeigned love. Our works are good when the will of God is the rule of them, and the glory of God the end of them; when they are done in his strength, and for his sake; to him, and not to men. Regeneration, or the new birth, is a subject to which the world is very averse; it is, however, the grand concern, in comparison with which every thing else is but trifling. What does it signify though we have food to eat in plenty, and variety of raiment to put on, if we are not born again? If after a few mornings and evenings spent in unthinking mirth, carnal pleasure, and riot, we die in our sins, and lie down in sorrow? What does it signify though we are well able to act our parts in life, in every other respect, if at last we hear from the Supreme Judge, “Depart from me, I know you not, you workers of iniquity?”

John was fully satisfied with the place and work assigned to him; but Jesus came on a more important work. He also knew that Jesus would increase in honor and influence, for there would be no end to his government and peace, while he himself would be less followed. John knew that Jesus came from heaven as the Son of God, while he was a sinful, mortal man who could only speak about the simpler subjects of religion. The words of Jesus were the words of God; he had the Spirit, not by measure as the prophets did, but in all fullness. Everlasting life can only be had by faith in Him, and it might be obtained thus; whereas all those who do not believe in the Son of God cannot partake of salvation, but the wrath of God forever rests upon them.
